_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Woodford County, KY. Formed 1789 from Fayette County. Records begin from formation, pre-statehood. No major courthouse fire. One of the most complete record sets in the Bluegrass.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by current building. No major fire.

record_losses.description: Woodford County has no record of a major courthouse fire. Records run continuously from the 1789 formation — pre-statehood records created under Virginia law. The county's wealth produced extensive records across all categories.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Woodford County after 1789, the records survive. Gaps reflect the ancestor's status, not courthouse losses.
record_losses.source: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate inventories from formation. Estate inventories listing enslaved people are extensive.
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in early loose papers.

vital_records.repository: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services; KDLA

substitute_sources.description: Distillery records — production logs, employee lists, barrel inventories — document workers in the bourbon industry. The Old Oscar Pepper Distillery (later Woodford Reserve) maintained production records from the early 1800s.
substitute_sources.repository: Kentucky Historical Society; Filson Historical Society; individual distillery archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies distillery workers and coopers. Production records establish presence at specific dates.

substitute_sources.description: The American Stud Book and Thoroughbred breeding farm records document not only horses but the people who bred, trained, and cared for them — including enslaved and free Black grooms and trainers.
substitute_sources.repository: Keeneland Library, Lexington; Kentucky Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies individuals associated with the horse industry, including Black horsemen whose skills were essential but whose names were rarely recorded in standard sources.

narrative_hooks: Woodford County's records are as well-preserved as any in the Bluegrass — if {ancestor_name} was here after 1789, the courthouse has the trail.

The distillery records on Glenn's Creek document workers the courthouse may miss — if {ancestor_name} was a cooper, distiller, or warehouse worker, the production logs may record what the deed books do not.

Thoroughbred breeding records at the Keeneland Library document the people behind the horses — including the Black grooms and trainers whose skills made the Bluegrass horse industry possible but whose names rarely appear in standard records.
Woodford County records begin under Virginia law — {ancestor_name}'s earliest deeds may cross-reference Virginia land grants filed in Richmond, not Versailles.
source_references: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.
Veach, Michael R. Kentucky Bourbon Whiskey. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2013.
Wall, Maryjean. How Kentucky Became Southern. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2010.
